preamble: The transformation of a palatal glide [j] into a voiced palatal stop [ɟ] is a classic example of fortition, often occurring in environments where glides are strengthened at the beginning of a syllable or word. This process, also known as consonantalization, is seen in the historical development of Romance and in languages like Kirundi.

examples.note: Initial stage of palatal glide strengthening in Early Romance.
Regular strengthening of glides in certain phonological contexts.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
